questions.question: Why are satellites advantageous for long-distance QKD compared to fiber?

options.text: Most of the path is vacuum with no absorption loss

questions.explanation: Unlike fiber where loss is exponential with distance, satellite links pass mostly through vacuum (no loss). Only the atmospheric portion (~10 km) contributes significant absorption, resulting in total loss that's manageable even over thousands of kilometers.

questions.question: What is the typical altitude for LEO (Low Earth Orbit) QKD satellites?
questions.explanation: LEO QKD satellites operate at 500-1000 km altitude. This provides a good balance between manageable link loss and sufficient coverage. Lower orbits have atmospheric drag issues; higher orbits have more loss.

questions.question: What is a major disadvantage of satellite QKD compared to fiber QKD?

options.text: Weather dependence and limited pass duration

questions.explanation: Satellite QKD is weather dependent (clouds block optical signals) and LEO satellites are only visible for ~5-10 minutes per pass. Fiber QKD works 24/7 regardless of weather, though with shorter range.

questions.question: In a downlink configuration, where is the photon source located?
questions.explanation: In downlink configuration, the photon source is on the satellite and detectors are on the ground. This is advantageous because atmospheric turbulence occurs after most of the beam propagation, reducing beam spreading effects.

questions.question: Why is GEO (geostationary orbit) challenging for current QKD systems?

options.text: The extreme distance (~36,000 km) causes very high loss
The atmosphere is thicker at GEO altitude

questions.explanation: GEO satellites are at 35,786 km altitude, causing ~60+ dB of geometric loss. This is currently too high for practical QKD with today's technology, though it would provide 24/7 coverage of a hemisphere.

questions.question: What security advantage does entanglement-based satellite QKD have over prepare-and-measure?

options.text: It doesn't require trusting the satellite

questions.explanation: With entanglement-based QKD, the satellite distributes entangled pairs to two ground stations. Security is verified by Bell inequality tests on the ground, so even a compromised satellite cannot learn the keys.

questions.question: Fiber QKD has higher loss per kilometer than satellite links at distances beyond ~100 km.
questions.explanation: True. Fiber has ~0.2 dB/km exponential loss. Beyond ~100-200 km, fiber loss exceeds satellite link loss because satellite links only have significant loss through the atmosphere (~10 km effective path), with the rest being vacuum.
